coredata.dc:title: A hollow-structured nanohybrid: Intelligent and visible drug delivery and photothermal therapy for cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lu
Hu, Bo
Liu, Ai-Hua
Zhang, Yang
coredata.dc:description: It is of great valuable in drug delivery to fabricate multifunctional nanovehicle for cancer therapy. Herein, hollow-structured hCu2-xS@Au nanoshell/satellite composite with doxorubicin-carrying was designed and synthesized for intelligent and visible drug delivery and satisfactory photothermal therapy. By modification of disulfide linkage bridged Au nanoshell and multi-carboxylic graphene quantum dots (MC-GODs) on the surface of hCu2-xS@Au nanoparticles, both the high concentration of glutathione and low pH in cancer cell/tissue can induce responsive drug release. The satisfactory photothermal conversion efficiency (32%) of hCu2-xS@Au@MC-GODs under 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation ascribed to the reduced bandgap and more circuit paths for electron transitions for hCuS modified with Au nanoparticles depend on density functional theory, which antitumor therapy efficacy was greatly enhanced by combining chemo- and photothermal therapy. Moreover, the fluorescence of MC-GODs was quenched/“turn off” as linking to the surface of hCu2-xS@Au, and also restored/“turn on” as the MC-GODs detaching from the surface of hCu2-xS@Au. The fluorescent switch of MC-GODs can serve as both a controllable drug release “guard” and “eyes” for visualized monitoring. The multi-modality therapy with controllable drug delivery, visual monitoring and high photothermal conversion efficiency may be anticipated by this versatile strategy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Drug delivery
Controllable release
Photothermal
Fluorescent tracer
Cancer therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Condition-induced release of loads in vitro
In vitro drug release and cytotoxicity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of hCu2-xS@Au (A), TEM images of hCu2-xS@Au (B) and high resolution XPS spectrum of ss-hCu2-xS@Au (C–H).

(A) UV–Vis–NIR spectra of hCuS, hCu2-xS@Au, hCu2-xS@Au–COOH and ss-hCu2-xS @Au. (B) Photothermal performance of hCu2-xS@Au (800 μg mL−1), and PBS (10 mM) under different 808 nm laser irradiation (0.8, 1.4 and 2.0 W cm−2) for 8 min. (C) Photothermal performance of hCu2-xS@Au of different concentrations (100, 400 and 800 μg mL−1) under 808 nm laser with power density of 2.0 W cm−2.(D) The photothermal conversion cycling test of hCu2-xS@Au suspension under 808 nm laser with power density of 2.0 W cm−2.

(A) TEM images of MC-GODs, inset: size distributions and HRTEM images of MC-GODs; (B) AFM images of MC-GODs, inset: height profile along the line from (a) to (b).(C) FTIR spectra of MC@GODs; (D) UV–Vis–NIR spectrum; (E) Fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelength; (F) Fluorescence spectra of primary MC-GODs, supernatant after interaction with ss-hCu2-xS@Au, hCu2-xS@Au@MC-GODs and detached MC-GODs from surface of ss-hCu2-xS@Au.
Drug release of hCu2-xS@Au-DOX without MC-GODs guards (A) and hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs (B) at pH 5.5, 7.4 and 8.5.

(A) Controllable release profile of DOX from hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs at pH 5.5 with the variation of additive concentration within a range of 0.5–20 mM; (B) Cell viability after incubation with hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs at different concentrations for 24 h. The HeLa cells were pretreated with 5 mM GSH and 10 mM GSH to increase the intracellular level of GSH, with un-pretreated HeLa cells as a control; (C) Relative viabilities of the HeLa cells after incubation with various concentrations of DOX; (D) Relative viabilities of the HeLa cells after incubation with hCu2-xS@Au@MC-GODs for 24 h. The incubated condition was 37 °C, 5% CO2.
Cytotoxicity assays of HeLa cells in the presence of hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@ MC-GODs (1–200 μg mL−1) with or without NIR irradiation at an irradiation energy level of 1.5 W cm−2 for 30 min.

Fluorescent micro-images of HeLa cells cultivated with 100 μg mL−1 of hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs (A), 200 μg mL−1 of MC-QODs (B) and 0.5 μg mL−1 of free DOX (C) for 2, 4, 6 h, respectively. The cell nucleus colored with DAPI was excited at 405 nm (A-a,e,i), (B-a,e,i,m) and (C-a,d,g,j). The DOX was excited at 561 nm (A-c,g,k) and (C-b,e,h,k) and the MC-GODs were excited at 488 nm (A-b,f,j) and (B-b,f,j,n). The images of (A-d,h,i) and (B-d,h,i,p) were the overlay of DAPI, MC-GODs, and DOX. (C-c,f,i,l) was the overlay of DAPI and DOX. The images were taken with the same voltage, offset and pinhole.
Schematic illustrating the preparation of the intelligent multifunctional theranostic platform (hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs).

head.ce:title: A hollow-structured nanohybrid: Intelligent and visible drug delivery and photothermal therapy for cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, School of Life Science, China Medical University, Shenyang, 110122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology, School of Life Science, China Medical University, Shenyang 110122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is of great valuable in drug delivery to fabricate multifunctional nanovehicle for cancer therapy. Herein, hollow-structured hCu2-xS@Au nanoshell/satellite composite with doxorubicin-carrying was designed and synthesized for intelligent and visible drug delivery and satisfactory photothermal therapy. By modification of disulfide linkage bridged Au nanoshell and multi-carboxylic graphene quantum dots (MC-GODs) on the surface of hCu2-xS@Au nanoparticles, both the high concentration of glutathione and low pH in cancer cell/tissue can induce responsive drug release. The satisfactory photothermal conversion efficiency (32%) of hCu2-xS@Au@MC-GODs under 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation ascribed to the reduced bandgap and more circuit paths for electron transitions for hCuS modified with Au nanoparticles depend on density functional theory, which antitumor therapy efficacy was greatly enhanced by combining chemo- and photothermal therapy. Moreover, the fluorescence of MC-GODs was quenched/“turn off” as linking to the surface of hCu2-xS@Au, and also restored/“turn on” as the MC-GODs detaching from the surface of hCu2-xS@Au. The fluorescent switch of MC-GODs can serve as both a controllable drug release “guard” and “eyes” for visualized monitoring. The multi-modality therapy with controllable drug delivery, visual monitoring and high photothermal conversion efficiency may be anticipated by this versatile strategy.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a serious threat to global public health, and presents the trend that increasing steadily year by year [1,2]. Chemotherapy is still one of the popular strategies ascribing to the relative high curative effect and diversity of drug-carrying [3–5]. It is of great importance in drug delivery to fabricate multifunctional nano-delivery systems with intelligent responsive properties for special tumor microenvironment such as low pH, high concentration of reducing substances such as glutathione (GSH) and anoxia characteristic [6,7]. As we know, the pH value in tumorsis generally more acidic (<6.8) than that in normal cells/tissues (~7.4), particularly, the pH value in endosome and lysosome is found to be even lower (5.0–5.5) [8]. In addition, thousands times of the redox potential difference between the extra-cellular and intracellular surroundings can be used to design the functional unit for stimuli-responsive delivery [9]. The intelligent systems can be activated and cargo released by varying the intracellular environment (e.g., pH or redox) or external physical signals (e.g., light, magnetic field), which can avoid the incipient leakage of drugs and reduce injury to normal cells/tissue in varying degrees. In our previous work, we developed a redox-sensitive system in which fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) served as gate-keepers grafted at the pore openings of mesoporous carbon nanoparticles by cleaving disulfide bonds [10]. Following a pH and redox responsive double-trigger, targeted continuous drug release nanocarrier was also developed and investigated [11,12]. Based on the previous studies, the multi-stimuli-responsive platform usually exhibited more flexible and controllable properties than mono-stimulus responsive or non-responsive system, which benefited for controllable drug release and enhanced the therapy efficiency.

In the past few years, phototherapy, including photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) as a typical noninvasive method with many advantages including remote controllability, target selectivity, and lower side effects. Adopted NIR light has advantages as being less harmful to normal tissues, deeper penetration, and higher precise spatial/temporal resolution of body tissue. As to PTT, the applied photo-thermal agents (PTAs) can generate hyperthermia rapidly under laser irradiation (such as 808 nm laser), which are beneficial for reducing laser power and lessening the damage to normal tissues [13]. The generated hyperthermia can effectively induce the tumor cell apoptosis via denaturation of membrane protein or changing the gene expression [14,15]. Notably, the multifunctional PTAs can also act as the vehicles of drugs for chemotherapy and treatment unit as they accumulate in tumor tissues (such as inorganic nanoparticles). Recently, in comparison with single mode, many reports have been proved that the chemotherapy in combination with PTT could markedly enhanced therapy efficiency [16–22]. Inorganic nanomaterial is one of the most investigated NIR PTAs, including tailor-shape noble metal nanostructures (gold nanorods, gold nanocages, etc.) and semiconductor nanoparticles (copper chalcogenide semiconductors) ascribe to their localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [23]. In addition, semiconductor CuS nanoparticles are a class of outstanding promising photothermal agents attributing to the low-cost, biodegradability, high photostability and low cytotoxicity, and the d-d energy band transition of Cu2+ ions endowing them with NIR light absorption [24]. Particularly, it is worth to investigate hybrid heterojunctions consisting of special shaped plasmonic metal and semiconductor nanoparticles, which have attracted more attention in synergistically improving PTT. Generally, noble metal nanoparticles are usually highly sensitive to their surrounding environment, and the combination with semiconductor nanoparticles could enhance the LSPR field. Moreover, the combination is capable of changing the electron transitions in the semiconductor domain and further improving the photothermal conversion efficiency [25]. Jiang's group synthesized a hybrid nano-structure AuCu9S5 composed of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) coated with Cu9S5, which showed enhanced optical absorption cross-section and high photothermal conversion efficiency in the second NIR window. As a result, Cu9S5 LSPR was enhanced by 50% in the presence of Au [26]. Each treatment has its own advantages, however, single therapy method alone is still not sufficing for complex tumors whenever chemotherapy or phototherapy. Hence, the combination of multimodal therapy strategies provides a synergistic effect and enhance the cancer therapy efficiency is thus urgently required.

Here, we reported a kind of drug loaded hollow-structured sulfide copper based nanoshell/satellite composite (hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs) as multifunctional cancer therapy platform. The hollow structure of hCu2-xS@Au not only improved the photothermal conversion efficiency under 808 nm laser irradiation, but also provided the large cavity for drug loading and release. After capping with MC-GODs by the disulfide linkage, endowing the system dual stimuli-responsive (pH/redox) properities, meanwhile, the nano-guard of fluorescent MC-GODs could be autonomously activated and release cargos when exposed to a higher concentration of intracellular glutathione, which visible monitoring of drug release process could be realized. These synergistically unshielding actions including chemotherapy and PTT made hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs hybrids presented excellent potential and could be anticipated for multimodal cancer therapy.

Ammonium sulfide [(NH4)2S] aqueous solution (20 wt%), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP K30), hydrazine anhydrous solution (N2H4·H2O) potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sodium nitrite (NaNO2) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). graphene oxide (GO), terephthalic acid (TPA), hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) (HAuCl4·3H2O), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), potassium hydroxide (KOH) and other common solvents and reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). DMEM (high glucose), trypsin, penicillin/streptomycin and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were obtained from Thermo Scientific (Logan, Utah, USA). 4′, 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), glutathione (GSH) and doxorubicin (DOX) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). The 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay kit was received from Nanjing KeyGEN Biotech (Nanjing, China). Other chemicals used in this study were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). These reagents were at least of analytical-reagent grade and used as received without further treatment. Ultra-pure water was used throughout the experiments.

X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) were obtained on a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) using Cu K a radiation. Scan electron microscopic (SEM) images were taken on a ZEISS Ultra/Plus scanning electronmicroscope (ZEISS, Germany). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were recorded on a JEM-2100 F microscope (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV. Zeta potential measurement of the nanomaterials in aqueous solution was measured on a ZEN3600 Nano Zetasizer (Malvern, UK). The photothermal properties of the materials were conducted by irradiating with a diode infrared laser. UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra were recorded by using a U-3900 UV spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were performed on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) providing a 0.5 cm quartz cuvette. Cell imaging was performed on a confocal microscope (Nikon Al, Nikon, Japan). The X-ray photo electron spectra (XPS) tests were conducted on a VG ESCALAB MK II electron spectrometer by using Mg Kα (1200 eV).

Hollow CuS nanoparticles (hCuS) were synthesized according to the previously reported method [24] with minor modification. Firstly, Cu2O nanospheres were prepared by adding N2H4⋅H2O into a solution of Cu2+ and NaOH (in 2-propanol) using PVP K30 as capping agent. In a typical synthesis, CuCl2⋅2H2O (0.1 mM) was dissolved in 60 mL 2-propanol, followed by added 0.2 g PVP K30 and NaOH solution in 2-propanol, the concentration of NaOH was 1 mM. After stirring for 10 min, 175 μL of N2H4⋅H2O (50 wt%) was added to the above mixture slowly and stirred for 10 min. With the addition of N2H4⋅H2O, the mixture immediately displayed orange Cu2O precipitates. The obtained Cu2O products were centrifuged, washed three times with water, respectively. All of above experiments were conducted at room temperature. Secondly, a stoichiometric amount of (NH4)2S aqueous solution was added into a well-dispersed Cu2O aqueous suspension, and the mixture was stirred for 1 h at room temperature. The products were collected through centrifugation, washed by deionized water and anhydrous ethanol for several times, and then freeze drying for standby application.

The hCu2-xS@Au nanospheres were synthesized. Briefly, 1.0 mL of the above-prepared hCuS suspension was dispersed in 1.2 mL ethanol, followed by the addition of 0.01 g PVP-K30. After stirring for 30 min, 9.6 mL HAuCl4·3H2O (0.3 mM) aqueous solution was added and the mixture was stirred for 10 min. Afterwards, 0.5 mL NaBH4 (3 mM) was added and the mixture was stirred for another 30 min. The products were collected by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 15 min and washed with ethanol.

1 mg hCu2-xS@Au was dispersed in 2 mL anhydrous ethanol, and 12.5 mg mercaptosuccinic acid was added and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Afterwards, the obtained product hCu2-xS@Au–COOH was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and washed with water. 50 mg hCu2-xS@Au–COOH was dispersed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH 5.5, 10 mM), and the carboxyl group was activated for 20 min by adding 10 mg EDC and 10 mg NHS, simultaneously. Afterwards, with the addition of 100 mg cystamine dihydrochloride, the pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 7.5 by using the NaOH solution (1 mol L−1) under vigorous stirring for 3 h at room temperature. The final product was centrifuged and washed by water, which was denoted as ss-hCu2-xS@Au for future use.

Graphene quantum dots (GODs) were synthesized by the following method [27]. Briefly, 0.1 g of GO was dispersed into 10 mL water containing 0.56 g of KOH by ultrasonic. Then, 0.015 g of TPA was added into 2 mL above GO dispersion. Afterwards, 3.0 mL H2O2 aqueous solution (0.5 mL 30% H2O2 and 2.5 mL H2O) was added, and then the mixture was transferred into stainless autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 5 h. The products were filtered through a 0.1 μm microporous membrane, rotary evaporated to removed unreacted H2O2, following adjusted to pH 7 and dialyzed (with molecular weight cutoff membranes 100–500 Da) for 3 days. Finally, the obtained MC-GODs was quantified after lyophilization and redispersed in water for further use.

2.0 mg ss-hCu2-xS@Au was added into free DOX solution (1.0 mL, 200 mg L−1), and the mixtures were shaken for 6 h at room temperature under dark conditions. The precipitate was separated by centrifugation and loosely retained DOX was washed off by water several times until the supernatant became colorless. The system of loading DOX was denoted as hCu2-xS@Au-DOX. Afterwards, the hCu2-xS@Au-DOX was re-dispersed in 1 mL buffer solution containing 500 μL PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) and 500 μL MC-GODs (1 mg mL−1). The mixed solution was shocked for another 6 h to block the entrance to prevent the leakage of DOX. All supernatants were collected for quantitative analysis and the actual drug loading content of DOX was calculated on basis of the absorbance at 482 nm. The DOX loading efficiency (LE) can be calculated by equation (1):
(1)
L E
( % )
=
m
D O X
−
m
u D O X
m
C
u
2 − X
S @ A u @ M C − G O D s
× 100 %
where m
DOX , m
uDOX and m
Cu2-xS@Au@MC-GODs are the mass of original DOX, unadsorbed DOX and hCu2-xS@Au@MC-GODs, respectively.

To investigate the photothermal behavior of ss-hCu2-xS@Au composites with different concentration, 1 mL of the aqueous solutions of ss-hCu2-xS@Au with different concentrations (100, 400, 800 mg L−1) were illuminated by an 808 nm continuous-wave laser at a power density of 2 W cm−2, then the photothermal effect of ss-hCu2-xS@Au (800 mg L−1) was estimated at different power density of 0.8, 1.4, 2 W cm−2, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition-induced release of loads in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the release of DOX, hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs was dispersed in 2 mL PBS solution with various pHs and concentration of GSH under continuous incubating at 37 °C. The mixture was centrifuged at different time interval, e.g., 2, 4, 8, 12, 24, 36, 48 h. 500 μL supernatant was taken out for measuring the amount of released DOX, and then the isometric of fresh PBS solution was added into the residual solution. The cumulative release of DOX was calculated as a percentage with respect to the loaded DOX within the delivery system.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of hCu2-xS@Au@MC-GODs, the intestinal epithelial cells and HeLa cells were chosen as normal cells and tumor cells model, respectively. The standard MTT cell assay was used on the HeLa cells, which were cultured in a humidified incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2. The HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plates for 20 h. Then different concentrations of hCu2-xS@Au@MC-GODs (1, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 mg L−1) were added to the medium, and the cells were incubated in 5% CO2 at 37 °C for 20 h, MTT (10 mL, 5.0 mg mL−1) was added followed by further incubation for 4 h. After removing the medium, 200 mL of DMSO was added and the absorbance was monitored at 490 nm. Similarly, the MTT cell assay applied to intestinal epithelial cells and cultivated for 36 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Different concentrations of hCu2-xS@Au@MC-GODs were added and kept incubation for 32 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. The other treatments were the same as for HeLa cells.

The responsive release performane of the nanovehicle was further evaluated by gradually increasing the co-incubation time for hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs and HeLa cells. Typically, HeLa cells were cultured in a confocal dish and grown for 24 h and then the medium was replaced with fresh DMEM containing 50 mg mL−1 hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs. The cells were washed with PBS, immobilized by paraformaldehyde (m/v, 4%), and stained for 3 min by DAPI (2 mg mL−1). The subcellular localization and intracellular DOX release from the nanovehicle at 2 h, 4 h, and 6 h are visualized with a confocal microscope.

The major procedures for synthesis of multifunctional system and the antitumor mechanism were described in Scheme 1 . First, the hollow-structured hCuS was synthesized, and the individual gold nanoparticles were deposited on the surface of hCu2-xS in situ by the reduction of NaBH4. Meanwhile, a portion of hCuS was reduced to Cu2S to form hCu2-xS@Au nanostructure. Then the thioglycollic acid was modified on the surface of hCu2-xS@Au by Au–S bond, followed by the modification of cysteine through the amidation between the carboxyl from the thioglycollic acid and the amino from cysteine to construct an intelligent carrier with quick-witted disulfide bond (ss-hCu2-xS@Au). After loaded by DOX, the MC-GODs capped the surface of ss-hCu2-xS@Au to construct the intelligent multifunctional theranostic platform (hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs).

As shown by the SEM and TEM images, the as-prepared hCu2-xS@Au nanoparticles exhibited hollow structure with the average diameter of around 100 nm (Fig. 1 A and B). The spherical gold nanoparticles were uniformly deposited on the surface of hCu2-xS to form the nanoshell. For confirming the formation of hCu2-xS@Au, the XRD patterns of hCuS and hCu2-xS@Au were shown in Fig. S1, the diffraction peaks at angles (2θ) of 29.23°, 31.78°, 32.85, 47.94°were corresponding respectively with the crystal faces of (1 0 2), (1 0 3), (0 0 6) and (1 1 0) of hCuS standard spectrum (JCPDS 06–0464). In comparison of hCuS, the obvious three peaks at angles of 37.32°, 48.35°and 48.38° was observed in the hCu2-xS@Au XRD patterns, and there are no differences with the standard spectrum of Cu2S (JCPDS 33–0490) ascribing to part of Cu2+ was reduced to Cu+ during the formation of gold nanoparticles. The obvious four peaks at angles (2θ) of 38.18°, 44.39°, 64.58° and 77.55° were assigned to crystal faces (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0) and (3 1 1) of Au nanoparticles (JCPDS 04–0784). The XPS spectra were shown in Fig. 1C–H, the XPS of ss-hCu2-xS@Au presented the typical Cu(0) or Cu(II) valence states, which were from the two peaks of Cu2p 1/2 at 951.2 eV and Cu2p 3/2 at 931.2 eV Au4f located at the 83.6 eV and 87.2 eV, the peaks from trace amount of Au4f 7/2 and Au4f 5/2. In addition, two strong peaks of S2p at 162.7 eV and 161.6 eV, which were belong to the S–C and S–Cu bonds, and the binding energy of S–S and S–Au bonds were at 163.7 eV and 161.2 eV. Apart from three peaks assigning to C1s, involving the CO bond at 288 eV, C–S bond at 286.6 eV, C–N bond at 285 eV and C–C bond at 284.6 eV. There were clearly displaying two peaks at 531.6 eV and 532.9 eV, corresponding to the typical CO and C–O bonds, and the N1s had the rise high at 399.45 eV was caused by C–N bonds. All of those demonstrated the successful growing of Au nanoshell and assembly of disulfide bond onto the surface of hCu2-xS@Au.

Generally, plasmonic nanoparticles can obtain the optimal photothermal effect as the SPR occurred resonance with the incident light, in other words, the stronger absorption of the incident light endowed higher photothermal activity [28,29]. The UV–Vis–NIR absorption spectra of the as-prepared hCuS, hCu2-xS@Au and derivatives (hCu2-xS@Au–COOH and ss-hCu2-xS@Au) were shown in Fig. 2 A. It can be seen that the absorption intensity of hCu2-xS@Au nanoparticles was markedly maintained compared with the individual hCuS from UV to NIR region (200–1200 nm), even for the derivatives of hCu2-xS@Au. Furthermore, in comparison of hCu2-xS@Au, the absorption peak slightly red-shifted after coating with organic active groups, ascribing to the formation of dielectric monolayer around the surface of hCu2-xS@Au after modification [30–32]. Additionally, it distinctly indicated that nanomaterials exhibited a strong absorption in NIR region, which guaranteed NIR laser causing PTT application as a promising photothermal therapy agent.

To further demonstrate the photothermal conversion properties of hCu2-xS@Au, the continuous wave laser source with an 808 nm wavelength was used to perform the temperature increment tests. The temperature elevation (ΔT, compared with initial value) of hCu2-xS@Au (concentration of 800 μg mL−1) under 808 nm laser irradiation with different energy density 0.8, 1.4, 2.0 W cm−2 was exhibited in Fig. 2B, PBS solution was as control under the same conditions. The temperature of hCu2-xS@Au aqueous solution increase by 31.9 °C under 808 nm laser irradiation in 2.0 W cm−2 for 8 min, and the improvement of 28.3 °C for the hCuS (Fig. S2A), while that of PBS only have slightly increase, and trend of gradual increase exhibited as improving the energy density. In addition, the temperature of hCu2-xS@Au aqueous dispersion with different concentration (100, 400, 800 μg mL−1) increased by 24.2 °C, 27.5 °C, 31.9 °C (ΔT, compared with initial value) under laser irradiation with the power density of 2.0 W cm−2 for 8 min (Fig. 2C), and the corresponding temperature of hCuS was 23.5 °C, 25.9 °C, 30.3 °C (Fig. S2B), respectively. Moreover, the photothermal effect of hCu2-xS@Au still maintained well after five cycles of heating and cooling (heating 8 min and cooling 22 min for one cycle (Fig. 2D). The corresponding photothermal conversion efficiency of hCu2-xS@Au (800) was 32.03%, and calculated by equation (2) [33]:
(2)
η =
hs
(
T
m a x
−
T
s u r r
)
−
Q
d i s
I
(
1 −
10
A 808
)
where the η is photothermal conversion efficiency, T
max represents the equilibrium temperature, T
surr is ambient temperature of the surroundings. The Q
dis represents heat loss from light absorbed by the container, which is calculated to be approximately 0 mW. I represent incident laser power (2.0 W cm−2), A
808 is the absorbance of nanomaterials at 808 nm. The hs is calculated using the following eqution (3):
(3)
τ s
=
m D
c D
h s
Where h represents heat transfer coefficient, s means the surface area of the container, m
D and c
D are the mass (0.8 mg) and heat capacity (4.2 J g−1) of the solvent.

TPA possesses typical benzene structure with two electron-withdrawing carboxyl groups, resulting in prone to interact on the defect sites of GODs via π−π conjugation interactions and repairing the defects of GODs [34–36]. The obtained MC-GODs inherited the intrinsic advantages of both the primary GODs and TPA molecule. The HR-TEM images of MC-GODs exhibited mono-dispersed and uniform particle size centered in 7.26 nm with almost spherical morphology (Fig. 3 A). The inset of Fig.3A confirmed a high crystallinity with an interplanar spacing of 0.210 nm. As expected, the result demonstrated the defects repaired and TPA conjugated enhanced the crystallinity of the composite. As shown in Fig. 3B, atomic force microscopy (AFM) analyses of MC-GODs illustrated an average height of 1.18 nm, corresponding to 2–3 graphene layers [37].

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra exhibited the component of TPA@GODs (Fig. 3C). The broad absorption band at 3440 cm−1was ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H, and 1603 cm−1 was ascribed to the skeletal vibration of aromatic ring. Typically, the stretching vibration of CO was observed at 1689 cm−1, however, the position shifted to lower frequency, which was ascribed to the conjugation between the aromatic rings and CO bond [30]. The UV–Vis–NIR spectrum of MC-GODs exhibited an obvious absorption at 236 nm with a weak shoulder at 285 nm, which assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and n-π* transition of CO (Fig. 3D). These results confirmed the MC-GODs had been synthesized successfully. To be a satisfactory tracer, the fluorescence performance of MC-GODs was a significant indicator. As shown in Fig. 3E, MC-GODs exhibited an excitation-dependence along with an excitation/emission maximum of 310 nm/430 nm, resulted in “π−π stacking” interaction by the molecular aggregation of MC-GODs. Interestingly, the fluorescence was quenched as the MC-GODs anchored to the surface of ss-hCu2-xS@Au, however, the fluorescence recovered with adding scheduled concentration of GSH, which ascribed to the disintegration of disulfide bond and locked MC-GODs fell off from the nanocomposite (Fig. 3F). Hence, it could indicate and trace the drug release behavior as a therapy monitor. In addition, no obvious difference of MC-GODs fluorescence was observed as the pH increased from 5.5 to 8.5 (Fig. S3), which benefited for application in acidic tumor microenvironment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release and cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: DOX was selected as a model drug for cancer chemotherapy to evaluate the loading and controlled release behaviors of hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs nanohybrids. The actual loading ratio of DOX on the nanohybrids was calculated to be 28.4 wt%, as determined by the characteristic optical absorbance of DOX at 482 nm. The relatively high drug loading capacity could be ascribed to the hollow structure of hCu2-xS@Au nanohybrid and the negative surface potential −17.5 mV (Fig. S4) of ss-hCu2-xS@Au, both enhanced the storage and absorbance of positive DOX molecules. The DOX release behaviors of with caps (hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs) and without caps (hCu2-xS@Au-DOX) in PBS at different values of pH (5.5, 7.4 and 8.5) and different concentration GSH (0.5–20 mM) were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, an accumulation release ratio of over ca. 40% was exhibited for the DOX loaded hCu2-xS@Au without capping within a much shorter time of 4 h whenever at pH 5.5 of simulated tumor microenvironment or the pH 7.4 of simulated physiological environment, which could lead irreversible damage for the normal cell/tissue. To the contrary, all the accumulation release ratio of less than 10% from the hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs composite was obtained within 48 h, and the release was virtually not affected by the variation of acidity whenever in acidic or alkaline media, especially at a physiological environment (pH 7.4) (Fig. 4B). This dramatic observation clearly illustrated that the MC-GODs guarded effectively prevented leakage of DOX in advance and refrained from the toxicity or adverse effect for normal cell/tissue.

On the other hand, the surface-modified redox sensitive disulfide linkage collapsed and integrity of the DOX-loaded nanohybrid was disintegrated, which accelerated the inside drug release (Fig.5A ). Generally, in the plasma of healthy people, the average concentration level of GSH is much lower level (ca. 2 μM) than in tumor cells (ca. 10 mM), because of rapid enzymatic degradation in the normal plasma. As a result, the drug DOX was still encapsulated in the cavity as the hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs distributed in the normal plasma. In contrast, a continuous release of DOX was triggered as hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs placed in the tumor microenvironment, and the release ratio could reach up to 92.8% in the presence of 20 mM GSH in 12 h. Furthermore, most of drugs releasing could be readily achieved within 8 h, it just met the needs of clinical administration medication. For further investigation, the therapeutic efficacy of the hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@ MC-GODs nanohybrid was estimated in vitro by quantifying the cell viability of HeLa cells using an MTT assay. In details, HeLa cells were incubated with hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs for 24 h. As shown in Fig. 5B, hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs exhibited obvious cytotoxicity and kill efficiency for unpretreated HeLa cells, however, the unloading DOX of hCu2-xS@Au@MC-GODs had an outstanding biocompatibility, even with a high concentration of 200 μg mL−1, whenever to HeLa cells (Fig. 5D) or to normal intestinal epithelial cells (Fig. S5). To verify our hypothesis more convincingly, 5 mM and 10 mM exogenous GSH as enhancer was beforehand incubated with HeLa cells for 2 h, then the HeLa cells were incubated with hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@ MC-GODs for 24 h. Interestingly, higher cytotoxicity was obtained for GSH pretreated than the un-pretreated HeLa cells with the same conditions, and higher concentration of GSH pretreatment provides an even higher cytotoxic efficacy, which was ascribed obviously to the release of more DOX inside the nanovehicle with increasing GSH after the uptake of the hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs. These results further indicated that the disulfide cross-linked hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs was a promising redox responsive nanovehicle for drug delivery.

For the purpose of performing chem- and photothermal therapy synergistically by using the nanoparticles, in vitro cytotoxicity test under NIR laser irradiation for 30 min and without NIR laser irradiation was further undertaken at different concentration of hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs. As depicted in Fig. 6 , when the cells were exposed to NIR irradiation, there were much more cells destroyed after being incubated with hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs than without NIR. This confirmed that the nanoparticles under laser irradiation NIR exhibited much more improved capability against cancer cells with respect to the case without NIR irradiation. As expected, the participation of phototherapy mechanism significantly enhanced the effect of synergistic treatment.

As mentioned in the previous sections, the nanovehicle of hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs gathered both the merits of redox-responsive controllable release and visual monitoring capability. Hence, cell imaging of hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs was conducted for the purpose of observing the drug release process and visualizing the localization of the nanovehicle inside the cells after internalization. Different incubation time (2 h, 4 h and 6 h) was conducted, with free DOX and MC-GODs as control for analyzing the phagocytosis and location in the cells. Cell nucleus was treated with DAPI and observed using a fluorescence micro-images system upon excitation at 405 nm, 488 nm and 561 nm, separately. It could be seen that red fluorescence was observed after incubation for 2 h, indicating that DOX was released from the vehicle (Fig. 7 C). The fluorescence intensity was intensified by increasing the incubation time from 2 h to 6 h, and DOX gradually passed through the nucleus membrane and eventually accumulated in the nucleus to against the cancer cells. On the contrary, the green fluorescence was represented at the region of cytoplasm for cells cultured with 200 μg mL−1 MC-GODs (Fig. 7B). As expected, after internalized into the cells (from 2 h to 6 h) of hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@ MC-GODs, strong red fluorescence (excited at 561 nm) in the whole cell and green fluorescence (excited at 488 nm) in the cytoplasm were represented (Fig. 7A), attributing to the cleavage of the disulfide bond by GSH resulted in the “turn on” of fluorescent MC-GODs, and the release of DOX from the vehicle. These results demonstrated that the stimuli-responsive DOX release and visible the cap fell off in the tumor microenvironment after being internalized into cells.

In summary, for the first time we have successfully synthesized a novel nanotherapy agent of hCu2-xS@Au-DOX@MC-GODs by integration of hCu2-xS@Au and tailoring fluorescent MC-GODs. The nanotherapy agent possessed excellent photothermal conversion efficiency as high as 32% under 808 nm NIR laser, attributing to the coupling between Au shell and hCu2-xS core. The disulfide bond as a bridge between hCu2-xS@Au and MC-GODs endowed the drug-delivery vehicle prominent capability of redox-responsive release. These enable the nanoparticles to be attractive as an intelligent candidate of drug-delivery vehicle and photothermal agent for cancer therapy. In addition, the traceable system has been constructed by introducing the fluorescent quantum dots and preventing them from the premature leakage of drug molecules, which effectively relieved the off-target toxicity for normal cells/tissues. Upon NIR and GSH triggering, the synergism of photothermal effect and chemotherapy played an uncommon role for enhancing HeLa cell uptake and a better cancer therapeutic effect.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Lu Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Bo Hu: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Ai-Hua Liu: Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Yang Zhang: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81673172, 21475144, 91227116 and 21605161), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017BB053) and Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (2018M642615) are highly appreciated.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

This supporting information section presented the XRD spectra patterns of hCuS and hCu2-xS@Au; Photothermal performance of hCuS (800 μg mL−1) and PBS (10 mM) under 808 nm laser irradiation with different power density (0.8, 1.4 and 2.0 W cm−2) for 8 min. Photothermal performance of hCuS of different concentrations (100, 400 and 800 μg mL−1) under 808 nm laser with power density of 2.0 W cm−2; Zeta potential of ss-hCu2-xS@Au in different pH (4−10) aqueous solution.
Multimedia Component1 Multimedia Component1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.120893.
